content: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria transform light energy into chemical energy. Chlorophyll captures sunlight, water is split, carbon dioxide is fixed into sugars, and oxygen is released as a by-product. Good educational questions should connect chloroplasts, light, carbon dioxide, glucose, and oxygen without implying that plants get food directly from soil.

The water cycle describes how water moves through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, infiltration, runoff, and collection. Solar energy drives evaporation, clouds form when water vapor cools and condenses, and gravity moves water back to rivers, oceans, and underground reservoirs. Strong questions should distinguish weather events from long-term climate patterns.

An algorithm is a finite, ordered set of steps for solving a problem. In computing, algorithms are evaluated by correctness, clarity, resource usage, and scalability. Sorting, searching, path finding, and recommendation are common examples. A good beginner question should avoid treating an algorithm as a programming language or a physical computer component.

A database stores structured information so it can be queried, updated, and protected. Relational databases organize data in tables with rows and columns, while document and vector databases optimize other access patterns. Vector databases store embeddings and retrieve items by similarity, which is useful for RAG systems that need relevant context rather than exact keyword matches.

The Industrial Revolution began in Britain during the eighteenth century and transformed production through mechanization, steam power, factory systems, and new transport networks. It changed work, urban life, global trade, and environmental impact. Balanced questions should include both technological innovation and social consequences.

The Cold War was a geopolitical rivalry after World War II, mainly between the United States and the Soviet Union. It involved nuclear deterrence, ideological competition, proxy conflicts, espionage, propaganda, and space exploration. The fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 symbolized the weakening of the Eastern Bloc but was not the only cause of the Soviet Union's collapse.

Plate tectonics explains how Earth's lithosphere is divided into moving plates. Divergent boundaries create new crust, convergent boundaries can form mountains or subduction zones, and transform boundaries slide past each other. Earthquakes and volcanoes often occur near plate boundaries, but not every earthquake is volcanic.

Climate zones group regions by long-term patterns of temperature, rainfall, and seasonality. Tropical, dry, temperate, continental, and polar climates influence ecosystems, agriculture, architecture, and human settlement. Climate is measured over long periods; it should not be confused with day-to-day weather.

Probability measures how likely an event is, usually from 0 to 1 or 0% to 100%. Independent events do not influence one another, while dependent events change later probabilities. Good questions should separate probability from certainty and should make sample space assumptions clear.

Geometry studies shapes, size, position, and spatial relationships. Triangles, circles, angles, perimeter, area, and volume are common beginner topics. The Pythagorean theorem applies to right triangles and relates the squares of the two shorter sides to the square of the hypotenuse.

Linear perspective is a drawing method that creates the illusion of depth on a flat surface. Parallel lines appear to converge at vanishing points on a horizon line. Renaissance artists used perspective to make architecture, rooms, and landscapes look spatially coherent.

Sports training improves performance through practice, recovery, technique, nutrition, and progressive overload. Aerobic training supports endurance, strength training builds force production, and flexibility work supports range of motion. Safe questions should avoid medical advice and focus on general principles.

Literary genres group works by shared conventions such as form, subject matter, tone, and reader expectations. Fiction includes novels and short stories, while poetry emphasizes rhythm, imagery, and compressed language. Drama is written for performance, and non-fiction presents factual or argumentative material. Good questions should distinguish genre from format and avoid assuming every book is a novel.

Narrative works are often analyzed through plot, character, setting, point of view, theme, and conflict. Plot describes the sequence of events, while theme refers to a broader idea or message explored by the work. First-person narration uses an 'I' perspective, whereas third-person narration describes characters from outside their direct voice. Strong educational questions should separate author, narrator, and character.

Video games combine rules, goals, feedback, interaction, and challenge into playable systems. Core gameplay loops describe repeated player actions such as exploring, collecting, solving, fighting, or building. Game mechanics are the rules and interactions available to players, while level design organizes spaces, obstacles, and pacing. Good questions should distinguish mechanics from graphics, story, and hardware.

Video game history includes arcade machines, home consoles, personal computers, handheld devices, and mobile platforms. Early arcade games popularized short skill-based sessions, while home consoles brought interactive entertainment into living rooms. Modern games often combine online services, digital distribution, accessibility features, and cross-platform play. Balanced questions should avoid treating one platform as the whole medium.
